content: The three-layer architecture (Framework / Domain / Executive-Assistant) was chosen to keep the framework entirely domain-agnostic. The framework in lib/ orchestrates, persists, and runs agents but contains no coding-specific knowledge. Each domain is a self-contained directory (domains/{id}/) that adds its own agents, prompts, capabilities, skills, extensions, and workflows. The executive-assistant layer (Layer 3) was explicitly deferred — it is not implemented and is reserved for a future always-on heartbeat agent.

Every domain must be a directory under domains/ containing a domain.ts manifest file. Without domain.ts the directory is silently skipped by loadDomains(). The fixed directory shape is: domain.ts (manifest), agents/ (*.ts definitions), prompts/ (*.md persona files), capabilities/ (*.md capability packs), skills/ (subdirectories), extensions/ (subdirectories), and optionally workflows.ts. Deviating from this layout breaks automatic discovery — the loader is non-recursive and indexes only the expected subdirectory names.

All agent IDs inside the framework use the qualified format {domain}/{agent} (e.g. coding/worker, coding/planner). Agent definitions are stored with qualified keys in AgentRegistry. Subagents allowlists in AgentDefinition must list qualified IDs. The runtime identity marker regex was updated to accept / in the capture group ([a-z0-9/-]+) so extractAgentIdFromSystemPrompt returns qualified IDs. For display only (e.g. ROLE_LABELS), strip the domain prefix — coding/worker renders as Worker.

System prompts are assembled in a strict four-layer order by assemblePrompts() in lib/domains/prompt-assembly.ts: Layer 0 — domains/shared/prompts/base.md (always); Layer 1 — per capability in agent.capabilities[], domain-first then shared fallback, error if missing in both; Layer 2 — domains/{domain}/prompts/{agent.id}.md (auto-loaded persona, not listed in capabilities); Layer 3 — domains/shared/prompts/runtime/sub-agent.md (only when spawned as a sub-agent). Layers are concatenated with \n\n. Do not manually list base or persona in capabilities — they are injected by convention.

All resource resolution (capabilities, skills, extensions) uses a two-tier domain-first then shared fallback pattern: check domains/{agent-domain}/{resource-type}/{name} first, then fall back to domains/shared/{resource-type}/{name}. Missing capabilities are errors; missing extensions are silently skipped. DomainRegistry.resolveCapability(name, preferredDomain) encapsulates this pattern for capability paths. Use it rather than constructing paths manually.

The shared domain is always loaded before all other domains. loadDomains() sorts discovered domain directories so shared comes first, then remaining domains alphabetically. This load order is critical because all other domains depend on shared capabilities and extensions being indexed first. Do not rely on filesystem readdir ordering — the explicit sort is the guarantee.

Moving extension directories from extensions/X/ to domains/shared/extensions/X/ adds two directory levels, which breaks every relative import inside the extension files. For example, ../../lib/agents/index.ts becomes ../../../../lib/agents/index.ts. Every extension's index.ts must have all its relative imports updated. This is not caught by TypeScript until runtime because the moved files still resolve at their new paths. Verify with bun run typecheck after any extension move.

After moving prompt files in TASK-055, subsequent agent spawns immediately failed because the Pi framework still resolved prompt slugs from the old prompts/ path. TASK-057 had to be retried twice before the root cause was identified. Lesson: when migrating prompt/skill/extension paths, update all consumers (especially the agent spawner and cli/session.ts) in the same operation as the file move. Never leave a window where old path constants and new file locations diverge.

The orchestration extension's spawn permission check (isSubagentAllowed) validates both the unqualified ID (targetDef.id, e.g. worker) and the qualified ID (${targetDef.domain}/${targetDef.id}, e.g. coding/worker) against the caller's subagents list. This allows subagents arrays written with either form to work during the transition period. Going forward, subagents arrays in agent definitions should use qualified IDs exclusively to avoid ambiguity.

Module-level registry singletons (DEFAULT_REGISTRY = createDefaultRegistry()) existed in five files: chain-parser.ts, chain-runner.ts, agent-spawner.ts, orchestration extension, and cli/main.ts. These were replaced with registry-as-parameter throughout, but createDefaultRegistry() was kept as a convenience bridge for tests and backward compat. The accepted trade-off: production code must always receive registry as a parameter; createDefaultRegistry() is acceptable in tests but should not be called in production paths.

Domain context for agent resolution follows a strict priority chain: (1) --domain CLI flag, (2) projectConfig.domain from .cosmonauts/config.json, (3) scan all domains with ambiguity error if the unqualified name matches agents in multiple domains. Code that resolves agents should always accept an explicit domain context parameter and propagate it rather than defaulting to scan-all.

AgentDefinition.capabilities replaces the old prompts array. The capabilities array lists only unqualified capability pack names (e.g. ["core", "tasks", "coding-readwrite"]) — never paths. The base prompt (base.md) and the agent persona prompt are NOT listed in capabilities; they are auto-injected by assemblePrompts() at layers 0 and 2. The domain field on AgentDefinition is set at runtime by the loader and must not appear in the static definition files.
